title: Remote POCUS Education Protocol for Article Screening and Selection
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"3n7je","text":"Abstract:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9},{"style":"UNDERLINE","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"errp7","text":"Background: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dmj7t","text":"Since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, learning at a distance has experienced a significant rise in popularity. Distance learning is a tool frequently used in medical education. Medical learning at a distance has the potential to reshape the field of medicine by allowing students from remote and underserved locations to more easily learn to provide care. There is a","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2vbuk","text":"need to better understand how distance learning plays a part in medical education. The use of point of care ultrasound to aid in the diagnosis and management of patients is well documented and becoming increasingly more prominent in medical education curriculums. Access to this valuable tool can be limited in remote and/or low and middle income countries. The use of remote learning may make POCUS more accessible. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"mfju","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"am7qv","text":"Objective: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fqt9f","text":"The objective of this scoping review is to examine the literature on remote point-of-care ultrasound education in lower- and middle-income countries to better understand what can be said about best practices and what outcomes are being studied. This scoping review aims to better understand the field of remote ultrasound education and identify gaps in the literature to inform future research.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8pitu","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9gdou","text":"Methods and analysis: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4v145","text":"The included articles were examined following the Scoping Reviews Joanna Briggs Institute scoping review methodological framework and the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis (PRISMA-ScR) protocol. A comprehensive search from a total of five (MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, CINAHL, Scopus) electronic databases was performed, beginning with MEDLINE to build a search with PubMed Medical Subject Heading (MeSH) terms and free terms built from synonyms of the MeSH terms. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c7bf2","text":"Then, the reference list of included articles was screened by 3 authors baseline on predetermined inclusion/exclusion criteria first by title/abstract then by full text. A calibration exercise was conducted with an initial batch of 10% of both title/abstracts and full text articles by all 3 authors. Two review authors independently. Articles were selected if two authors agreed. The\nextracted data will be qualitatively analyzed and presented in a diagrammatic or tabular form, alongside a narrative summary, in line with Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis: extension for Scoping Reviews reporting guidelines.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Initial ScreeningPlan
a. TW, SL, and EA review the title AND abstract forall articles - Reviewing title alone is insufficient - If abstract is unavailable, review the full text of the article
b. Remove irrelevant studies based on predeterminedinclusion/exclusion criteria
Initial Calibration
a. TW, SL, and EA all review title/abstract for a batch of 10% of the articles (230 articles)
c. If ≤ 90% agreement with first 10% of articles,TW, SL, EA should meet to discuss, clarify/consider modifying inclusioncriteria if needed
b. If > 90% agreement among reviewers withinitial 10% of articles, then proceed with screening remainder of articles
- if inclusion criteria are modified, selectanother 10% of articles (230 articles) for a second calibration exercise withmodified inclusion criteria
Screening of Remainder of Title/Abstracts
a. TW, SL, EA will each review remainder ofarticles (title and abstract, full-text if abstract not available)
b. Articles marked as not applicable by ≥ 2reviewers are excluded
Initial Calibration
a. TW, SL, and EA review full text for initialbatch of 10% of articles
a. If > 90% agreement among reviewers with initial10% of articles, then proceed with full-text review of all remaining articles
a. If ≤ 90%agreement with first 10% of articles, TW, SL, EA should meet to discuss, clarify/considermodifying inclusion criteria if needed
a. Reasons for exclusion will be documented in bothCovidence and study spreadsheet - Articles will be included if 2 or more reviewersagree
Review of remainder of articles
a. TW, SL, and EA review full-text articles - Remaining 90% of articles divided into thirds: TW/SL review 1/3, TW/EA review 1/3, SL/EA review1/3
b. For inclusion in review, 2 or more reviewersshould agree
Data Extraction
a. Group meets to discuss and refine dataextraction form after all 3 reviewers have each reviewed 5-10 papers and entereddata onto form
b. Reviewers add any additional data to form fromtheir previously reviewed articles
creator: Elisa A
